findings.title: Use of Non-Cryptographically Secure Random Number Generator for Token Generation
findings.description: The application uses System.Random for generating authentication tokens, which is not cryptographically secure and can lead to predictable token generation.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker who can observe multiple authentication tokens can use statistical analysis to predict future tokens. With enough observed tokens, they can forge valid authentication tokens and gain unauthorized access to user accounts.
findings.remediation: Replace System.Random with System.Security.Cryptography.RandomNumberGenerator or use the built-in Guid.NewGuid() method for generating secure tokens.

findings.title: Use of Non-Cryptographically Secure Random Number Generator for Password Generation
findings.description: The application uses System.Random for generating passwords, which is not cryptographically secure and can lead to predictable password generation.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker who can predict password generation patterns can brute-force or guess user passwords. This is particularly dangerous as these passwords are used for master password derivation and could lead to account takeover.
findings.remediation: Replace System.Random with System.Security.Cryptography.RandomNumberGenerator for generating cryptographically secure random passwords.

findings.title: Weak Key Derivation Process
findings.description: The key derivation process truncates or pads a Base64-encoded password to exactly 32 characters, which does not constitute a proper key derivation function (KDF).
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker can perform dictionary attacks against the truncated Base64 password, significantly reducing the entropy. This makes it easier to crack the master password and gain access to encrypted user data.
findings.remediation: Replace the current key derivation with a proper KDF such as PBKDF2, bcrypt, or scrypt with sufficient iterations and salt.

findings.title: Missing Authenticated Encryption in Symmetric Key Operations
findings.description: The application uses basic AES encryption without authenticated encryption (e.g., AES-GCM), making it vulnerable to padding oracle attacks and ciphertext manipulation.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker can modify encrypted data and observe how the application handles decryption errors, potentially extracting plaintext or forging valid ciphertext. This could allow access to encrypted user notes and other sensitive data.
findings.remediation: Switch to AES-GCM or AES-CCM mode which provides both confidentiality and authenticity. Replace the current encryption/decryption methods with authenticated encryption implementations.

findings.title: Insecure Direct Object Reference in Authentication Handler
findings.description: The authentication logic compares a decrypted token directly against the incoming token without ensuring that the comparison is constant-time.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker can perform timing attacks to determine if a token matches, potentially allowing them to infer valid tokens and escalate privileges or gain unauthorized access to user accounts.
findings.remediation: Use a constant-time string comparison method such as System.Security.Cryptography.HashAlgorithm.ComputeHash or a dedicated constant-time comparison function to prevent timing attacks.

findings.title: Wildcard CORS Configuration Allows Cross-Origin Requests
findings.description: The application allows requests from any host via wildcard configuration ('*'), exposing it to Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) misconfigurations.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker can craft a malicious website that makes requests to the API on behalf of authenticated users, potentially stealing tokens or performing unauthorized actions. This also opens the door to CSRF attacks.
findings.remediation: Restrict the AllowedHosts configuration to specific, known domains instead of using a wildcard. Configure CORS policies to only allow trusted origins.

findings.title: Hardcoded MongoDB Connection String in Development Settings
findings.description: The MongoDB connection string in development settings points to a hardcoded local Docker host address.
findings.attack_scenario: If this configuration is accidentally deployed to production, it exposes internal network details and database access. Even in development, it could be exploited by attackers with access to the development environment.
findings.remediation: Remove hardcoded connection strings from configuration files. Use environment variables or secure secret management systems for database connection strings in all environments.

findings.title: Insufficient PBKDF2 Iteration Count
findings.description: PBKDF2 iteration count of 10000 is considered low by modern standards and may be insufficient against brute-force attacks.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker with access to hashed passwords can perform brute-force attacks more efficiently than with higher iteration counts, making password cracking faster and more feasible.
findings.remediation: Increase the PBKDF2 iteration count to at least 100,000 or higher depending on performance requirements. Consider using bcrypt or scrypt for better security.

findings.title: Insufficient PBKDF2 Output Length
findings.description: PBKDF2 output length of 20 bytes is insufficient for modern security requirements.
findings.attack_scenario: With only 20 bytes of output, the hash has lower entropy and is more susceptible to collision attacks and brute-force attacks compared to modern standards.
findings.remediation: Increase the PBKDF2 output length to at least 32 bytes (256 bits) to meet modern security requirements. Use SHA-256 or higher for the hash function.

findings.title: Insecure Configuration in Production Environment
findings.description: Empty production configuration file suggests missing security configurations such as strict transport security headers.
findings.attack_scenario: Without proper security headers and configurations, the application is vulnerable to various attacks including XSS, clickjacking, and insecure communication. This reduces the overall security posture in production.
findings.remediation: Implement proper security headers in production configuration including Strict-Transport-Security, Content-Security-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options, and X-Frame-Options. Enable HTTPS enforcement and secure cookie settings.

findings.title: Deprecated RNGCryptoServiceProvider Usage
findings.description: Although RNGCryptoServiceProvider is used for generating salt, the code should prefer RandomNumberGenerator instead.
findings.attack_scenario: While not immediately exploitable, using the deprecated RNGCryptoServiceProvider instead of the newer RandomNumberGenerator is a best practice violation that could lead to compatibility issues or reduced security in future .NET versions.
findings.remediation: Replace RNGCryptoServiceProvider with RandomNumberGenerator, which is the recommended approach for cryptographic randomness in modern .NET applications.

findings.title: Potential Injection Vulnerability in Password Generation
findings.description: Removal of elements from a list based on user input without sanitization or validation can lead to unexpected behavior.
findings.attack_scenario: If an attacker can inject malicious input into the exclusion parameter, they might be able to manipulate the character set used for password generation, potentially creating predictable or weak passwords.
findings.remediation: Validate and sanitize all user inputs before processing. Implement a whitelist approach for allowed special characters rather than removing from a predefined list.
